text.title: Pyramid vs Trophy Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that translate the 5-question diagnostic into a defensible per-module test-shape decision.

rule.statement: The pyramid-vs-trophy decision is taken PER MODULE, not per team or per repo. Different modules in the same repo can have different shapes. The decision is recorded in test-strategy.yaml keyed by module name.

rationale.source: empirical analysis of mixed-shape suites; module-coupling research

rule.rationale: A pure-logic module (e.g. tax-calculation library) deserves pyramid (cheap unit tests catch real bugs). An orchestration module (e.g. checkout workflow) deserves trophy (integration tests catch wiring bugs that unit tests miss). Forcing one shape across both produces simultaneously brittle and shallow suites.

rule.statement: For each module, answer 5 questions: (1) % of code that is pure logic vs orchestration (0-100); (2) commits per month (data from git); (3) % of incidents in this module that were integration-related (data from incident log); (4) current unit-test runtime in seconds; (5) ACs predominantly at unit level (single function behavior) or workflow level (multi-step user flow). Answers map to pyramid / trophy / hybrid via the lookup table.
rule.rationale: A single-question heuristic over-fits. Five questions diagnose the module's character. The lookup table below makes the mapping reproducible across engineers.

example.title: Decision lookup table
example.code: question 1 (pure logic %) >= 60% AND question 5 (ACs unit-level) → PYRAMID question 1 < 30% AND question 5 (ACs workflow) → TROPHY question 3 (integration incidents) > 50% → TROPHY question 4 (unit runtime) > 300s AND question 2 (commits) > 30/mo → TROPHY or HYBRID all other combinations → HYBRID (case by case)

rule.statement: The chosen shape sets the dominant investment but does NOT veto the others. Pyramid module still has some integration tests; trophy module still has some unit tests. The shape is the proportion, not the exclusion. Forbidden: a module with zero of any test type unless explicitly justified.

rationale.source: testing-shape evolution research; observed failures of strict shape mandates

rule.rationale: Strict shape mandates produce blind spots ("we are pyramid, we do not write integration tests"). The shape is the default; exceptions exist for specific tests that need to live in the non-default tier.

rule.statement: Each shape has a runtime budget for PR-time CI: pyramid module = under 60 seconds of unit + 0-60 seconds of integration; trophy module = under 120 seconds of integration + 0-60 seconds of unit. Modules over budget either re-balance (move some tests to nightly), parallelise, or trim. Hard cap: 8 minutes total PR-time test runtime across all modules.
rule.rationale: PR-time runtime is the single biggest predictor of whether developers actually run tests locally. An 8-minute cap is the empirical sweet spot — beyond it, developers start merging without running tests, and the suite becomes decorative.

rule.statement: Per-module decisions are reassessed every quarter. Triggers for an out-of-cycle reassessment: a major architecture change (orchestration extracted to a separate service), an incident that the current shape missed, runtime budget breached for 2 consecutive months. Decisions are mutable; the discipline is to keep them current.

rationale.source: test-strategy maintenance research; common stale-decision patterns

rule.rationale: Test shape decisions made at module birth become wrong as the module evolves. A pure-logic library that becomes an integration hub needs a different shape. The quarterly reassessment ritual keeps decisions current.

list.item: Pyramid (Cohn 2009) — many unit tests, fewer integration, very few e2e. Best for pure-logic modules, libraries, calculation engines
Testing Trophy (Dodds 2018) — heavy integration tests, some unit, light e2e, static analysis at the base. Best for UI components, workflow orchestrators, service-to-service composition
Hybrid — explicit mix per module; the most common real-world shape but only defensible if the per-module diagnostic supports it
